sections.heading: This Act is the Personal Data Protection Act 2012.
sections.title: Act 26 of 2012—Personal Data Protection Act 2012
sections.summary: This section is a legislative reference stating that the Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 26 of 2012) was enacted as a bill in 2012 and came into effect on 2 January 2013 for most parts. It provides historical context for the Act's implementation timeline and commencement dates.
sections.source_quote: 1. Act 26 of 2012—Personal Data Protection Act 2012

sections.heading: The purpose of this Act is to govern the collection, use and disclosure of personal
sections.title: Business Names Registration Act 2014
sections.summary: This section references the Business Names Registration Act 2014 (Act 29 of 2014), which was enacted in 2014 and came into effect on 3 January 2016. It is unrelated to data protection and does not contain substantive obligations for data transfers.
sections.source_quote: 3. Act 29 of 2014—Business Names Registration Act 2014

sections.heading: The functions of the Commission are —
sections.title: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 operational timeline
sections.summary: This section specifies the operational timeline for the Personal Data Protection Act 2012, indicating that it came into effect on 31 December 2021. The section serves as a notice of when the Act was implemented after its initial enactment.

requirements.text: Operation: 31 December 2021

sections.source_quote: 2020 Revised Edition—Personal Data Protection Act 2012

sections.heading: An organisation must —
An organisation must not, on or after 2 July 2014, collect, use or disclose personal
sections.title: Consent requirements for personal data collection and use
sections.summary: Organisations must not collect, use or disclose personal data without the individual's consent or legal authorization. Consent must be explicitly given or deemed valid under this Act, with specific conditions for validity and withdrawal. The consent must be informed and not obtained through deceptive practices.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit or deemed consent for data processing, with specific conditions for validity and withdrawal. This section impacts DTIA by mandating consent mechanisms and procedures for data transfers.

requirements.text: Organisation must not collect, use or disclose personal data without individual consent or legal authorization
Individual must provide consent or be deemed to have given consent under this Act
Consent must be given in accordance with this Act after receiving required information
Organisation must not require consent beyond reasonable scope for products/services
Organisation must not obtain consent through false or misleading information

sections.source_quote: An organisation must not, on or after 2 July 2014, collect, use or disclose personal data about an individual unless — (a) the individual gives, or is deemed to have given, his or her consent under this Act to the collection, use or disclosure, as the case may be; or (b) the collection, use or disclosure (as the case may be) without the individual's consent is required or authorised under this Act or any other written law.
sections.relevance_reason: Core consent requirements for data collection

sections.heading: An organisation must not, on or after 2 July 2014, collect, use or disclose personal (cont.)
An organisation may collect, use or disclose personal data about an individual
Despite the other provisions in this Part, an organisation may use personal data
An organisation must make a reasonable effort to ensure that personal data
An organisation must protect personal data in its possession or under its control
sections.title: Organisation must protect personal data with reasonable security
sections.summary: Organisations must implement reasonable security measures to prevent unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, disposal, or similar risks to personal data they possess or control. They must also prevent the loss of storage media or devices containing such data. This obligation applies to all organisations handling personal data in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires organisations to implement security measures to protect personal data from breaches during storage or processing, directly impacting cross-border data transfers by ensuring data remains secure before transfer.

requirements.text: Organisations must implement reasonable security arrangements to prevent unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification or disposal of personal data
Organisations must prevent loss of storage media or devices containing personal data

sections.source_quote: 24. An organisation must protect personal data in its possession or under its control by making reasonable security arrangements to prevent — (a) unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification or disposal, or similar risks; and (b) the loss of any storage medium or device on which personal data is stored.
sections.relevance_reason: Core security obligation for personal data protection

sections.heading: An organisation must cease to retain its documents containing personal data, or
sections.title: Organisation must cease retaining personal data when purpose no longer applies
sections.summary: Organisations must stop retaining personal data or remove means to link it to individuals once it's no longer necessary for legal or business purposes, or when the original purpose for collection has ended. This applies to all organisations holding personal data in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires organisations to delete or anonymise personal data when it's no longer needed, which impacts data transfer by ensuring data is not retained beyond its purpose, reducing cross-border transfer risks.

requirements.text: Organisations must cease retaining documents containing personal data when the purpose for collection no longer serves retention
Organisations must remove means to associate personal data with individuals when retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes

sections.source_quote: 25. An organisation must cease to retain its documents containing personal data, or remove the means by which the personal data can be associated with particular individuals, as soon as it is reasonable to assume that — (a) the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served by retention of the personal data; and (b) retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes.

sections.heading: [Repealed by Act 40 of 2020]
This Part applies to a specified message addressed to a Singapore telephone
sections.title: Do Not Call Register applies to messages addressed to Singapore numbers
sections.summary: The Do Not Call Register applies to messages sent to Singapore telephone numbers when the sender is present in Singapore at the time of sending or the recipient is present in Singapore when the message is accessed. This defines the scope of the register for communication data.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when Do Not Call Register applies, which impacts DTIAs by determining which communication data transfers require registration.

requirements.text: Do Not Call Register applies to messages addressed to Singapore telephone numbers when sender is present in Singapore at time of sending or recipient is present in Singapore when message is accessed

sections.source_quote: 38. This Part applies to a specified message addressed to a Singapore telephone number where — (a) the sender of the specified message is present in Singapore when the specified message is sent; or (b) the recipient of the specified message is present in Singapore when the specified message is accessed.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: defines communication scope for Do Not Call Register

sections.heading: A certificate purporting to be signed by the Chief Executive of the Authority or an
sections.title: Certificate of Singapore telephone number status admissible as evidence
sections.summary: A certificate signed by the Chief Executive or authorized officer stating whether a Singapore telephone number was listed in a register at a specific date is admissible as evidence in legal proceedings. This section defines the admissibility of such certificates for terminated Singapore telephone numbers, which must meet specific criteria including allocation to a subscriber and termination by the subscriber or provider without reallocation.

requirements.text: A certificate signed by the Chief Executive or authorized officer stating a Singapore telephone number was or was not listed in a register at a specified date is admissible as evidence

sections.source_quote: A certificate purporting to be signed by the Chief Executive of the Authority or an authorised officer and stating that a Singapore telephone number was or was not listed in a register at a date specified in the certificate is admissible as evidence of its contents in any proceedings.
sections.relevance_reason: Purely procedural for telephone numbers, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(2), a person must not send a specified message addressed to
sections.title: Messages must include sender and contact information
sections.summary: A person cannot send a specified message to a Singapore telephone number without including clear identification of the sender, contact details for the sender, compliance with regulations, and ensuring the information remains valid for at least 30 days after sending.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures messages sent to Singapore numbers include necessary information for accountability and compliance, reducing risks of misuse.

requirements.text: Messages must include clear identification of the sender
Messages must include contact details for the sender
Messages must comply with regulations
Information must remain valid for at least 30 days after sending

sections.source_quote: A person must not send a specified message addressed to a Singapore telephone number unless the specified message includes clear and accurate information identifying the individual or organisation that sent or authorised the sending of the specified message.

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified
sections.title: Prohibition on concealing calling line identity
sections.summary: A person making a voice call containing a specified message to a Singapore telephone number must not conceal or withhold the calling line identity of the sender, nor perform operations to conceal the identity. This applies to both direct and authorized calls.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires transparency in calling line identity for Singapore numbers to prevent unauthorized communication and ensure accountability.

requirements.text: A person must not conceal or withhold calling line identity of sender
A person must not perform operations to conceal calling line identity

sections.source_quote: A person that makes a voice call containing a specified message... must not conceal or withhold from the recipient the calling line identity of the sender.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits calling line identity concealment for Singapore calls

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Voice call data sharing within employment context
sections.summary: This section specifies that voice call data containing specified information may be shared within an organization if it is done according to instructions given by the employee's employer during employment. It excludes employees who were officers or partners of the sender at the time of communication and for whom it can be proven they knew or reasonably should have known the telephone number was applicable and the message was sent with their consent or connivance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when voice call data can be shared internally within an organization without requiring additional safeguards for cross-border transfers, relevant for assessing internal data sharing within employment contexts.

requirements.text: Voice call data containing specified information may be shared within an organization if done according to instructions given by the employee's employer during employment
Excludes employees who were officers or partners of the sender at the time of communication and for whom it can be proven they knew or reasonably should have known the telephone number was applicable
Excludes employees who were officers or partners of the sender at the time of communication and for whom it can be proven the message was sent with their consent or connivance

sections.source_quote: in accordance with instructions given to P by or on behalf of P's employer in the course of P's employment. [40/2020]
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses employee voice call data sharing within employment context

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Voice call data sharing exceptions for employees
sections.summary: This section outlines exceptions where voice call data sharing within an organization is not permitted for employees who were officers or partners of the sender at the time of communication. It requires proof that the employee knew or reasonably should have known the telephone number was applicable and that the message was sent with their consent or connivance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides specific exceptions for when voice call data sharing within an organization is not permitted for employees, relevant for assessing internal data sharing within employment contexts.

requirements.text: Excludes employees who were officers or partners of the sender at the time of communication
Requires proof that the employee knew or reasonably should have known the telephone number was applicable
Requires proof that the message was sent with the employee's consent or connivance

sections.source_quote: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.) [40/2020]
sections.relevance_reason: Voice call data sharing exceptions for employees

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Procedure for financial penalty notices and representations
sections.summary: The Commission must provide written notice to an organisation or person before imposing a financial penalty, detailing the proposed action, instances of non-compliance, and a minimum 14-day period for written representations. The Commission may extend this period for exceptional circumstances. After considering representations or expiry of the period, the Commission may impose the penalty, serve notice on the organisation and complainant, and specify payment deadlines with interest rates not exceeding court judgment debt rates.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires written notice and 14-day representations before financial penalties; specifies payment deadlines and interest rates; relevant for assessing penalty procedures in cross-border data compliance contexts.

requirements.text: Commission must give written notice to organisation or person before imposing financial penalty
Notice must state Commission's intent to take action against organisation or person
Notice must specify proposed directions or financial penalties
Notice must specify instances of non-compliance or reasons for proposed action
Notice must specify minimum 14-day period for written representations
Commission may extend representation period for exceptional circumstances
Commission must serve notice of decision on organisation and complainant
Financial penalty payment deadline must be at least 28 days after notice
Commission may allow payment extensions or instalments for penalties
Interest on penalties must not exceed court judgment debt rates

sections.source_quote: Before giving any direction under section 48I or imposing a financial penalty under section 48J(1), the Commission must give written notice to the organisation or person concerned — (a) stating that the Commission intends to take action against the organisation or person under section 48I or 48J(1), as the case may be; (b) where the Commission intends to give any direction under section 48I, specifying the direction the Commission proposes to give; (c) specifying each instance of non-compliance that is the subject of the proposed action, or the reason or reasons for the proposed action; and (d) subject to subsections (2) and (3), specifying the time within which written representations may be made to the Commission with respect to the proposed action.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalty procedures for non-compliance with data protection

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Reconsideration procedures for enforcement decisions
sections.summary: Organisations or persons may request reconsideration of Commission decisions within a prescribed period, including grounds for reconsideration. The Commission may extend the reconsideration period for exceptional circumstances. The Commission must reconsider the decision, potentially affirm, revoke, or vary the decision, and notify the applicant of the outcome. Reconsideration does not suspend the effect of the decision except for financial penalty appeals.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides reconsideration mechanisms for enforcement decisions; relevant for assessing whether penalties or directions are justified in cross-border data compliance scenarios.

requirements.text: Organisation or person may make written application for reconsideration within prescribed period
Application must specify grounds for reconsideration
Commission may extend reconsideration period for exceptional circumstances
Commission must reconsider the decision and notify applicant of outcome
Commission may affirm, revoke, or vary the decision
Reconsideration does not suspend decision effect except for financial penalty appeals

sections.source_quote: An organisation or a person aggrieved by any direction made by the Commission under section 48G(2), 48I(1) or (2) or 48L(4) may make a written application to the Commission to reconsider the direction or decision in accordance with this section.

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Offence for unauthorized data access and modification
sections.summary: This section defines offences for unauthorized access, modification, or destruction of personal data records without proper authorization. It specifies that a person commits an offence if they make a request to obtain or change personal data without the individual's authority, or if they transmit personal data to another organization without authorization. The offences include specific penalties for individuals and organizations, with fines up to $50,000 for corporations and imprisonment terms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes strict conditions for data access and modification, requiring explicit authorization for personal data requests. It impacts DTIAs by mandating that organizations verify authorization before handling personal data, especially in cross-border contexts where data transfers may occur without proper consent.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they make a request under section 21(1) to obtain access to personal data without the individual's authority
A person commits an offence if they make a request under section 22(1) to change personal data without the individual's authority
A person commits an offence if they transmit personal data to another organization without the individual's authority
An individual who gives data porting requests in personal or domestic capacity is exempt from the above prohibition
A person guilty of an offence under subsection (1) faces a fine of up to $5,000 or imprisonment for up to 12 months
An organization committing an offence under subsection (3)(a) faces a fine of up to $50,000

sections.source_quote: A person shall be guilty of an offence if the person — (a) makes a request under section 21(1) to obtain access to personal data about another individual without the authority of that other individual; (b) makes a request under section 22(1) to change personal data about another individual without the authority of that other individual; or (c) subject to subsection (1A), gives a porting organisation a data porting request under section 26H(1) to transmit personal data about another individual to a receiving organisation without the authority of that other individual.

sections.heading: Subject to section 48(3), a person that makes a voice call containing a specified (cont.)
sections.title: Offence liability for unincorporated associations and partnerships
sections.summary: This section outlines the liability framework for unincorporated associations and partnerships that commit offences under the Act. It specifies that individuals involved in management or operations of such entities can be held liable for offences committed by the association or partnership, including consent, complicity, or failure to prevent misconduct. The section also includes provisions for the application of the rules to entities outside Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by clarifying liability for unincorporated entities, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving such organizations. It requires organizations to assess the liability of third parties when transferring data internationally.

requirements.text: A person involved in management of an unincorporated association or partnership can be held liable for offences committed by the entity
A person who consents or connives with others to commit an offence is liable
A person who knowingly fails to prevent an offence by the entity is liable

sections.source_quote: Where an unincorporated association or a partnership commits an offence under this Act, a person — (a) who is — (i) an officer of the unincorporated association or a member of its governing body; (ii) a partner in the partnership; or (iii) an individual involved in the management of the unincorporated association or partnership and in a position to influence the conduct of the unincorporated association or partnership (as the case may be) in relation to the commission of the offence; and (b) who — (i) consents or connives, or conspires with others, to effect the commission of the offence; (ii) is in any other way, whether by act or omission, knowingly concerned in, or is party to, the commission of the offence by the unincorporated association or partnership; or (iii) knew or ought reasonably to have known that the offence by the unincorporated association or partnership (or an offence of the same type) would be or is being committed, and failed to take all reasonable steps to prevent or stop the commission of that offence, shall be guilty of the same offence as is the unincorporated association or partnership (as the case may be), and shall be liable on conviction to be punished accordingly.
sections.relevance_reason: Liability for data offences in unincorporated entities

sections.heading: Despite any provision to the contrary in the Criminal Procedure Code 2010, a
sections.title: Jurisdiction for data protection offences
sections.summary: This section grants District Courts jurisdiction to try offences under the Act and impose full penalties, including fines and imprisonment. It ensures that data protection violations can be prosecuted in Singapore courts, regardless of the location of the offender or the data involved.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes the legal jurisdiction for data protection offences, which affects cross-border data transfers by determining where enforcement actions may occur.

requirements.text: District Courts have jurisdiction to try offences under this Act
District Courts have power to impose full penalties for offences

sections.source_quote: Despite any provision to the contrary in the Criminal Procedure Code 2010, a District Court has jurisdiction to try any offence under this Act and has power to impose the full penalty or punishment in respect of the offence.

sections.heading: A person guilty of an offence under this Act for which no penalty is expressly
sections.title: Penalties for offences without specified penalties
sections.summary: This section specifies penalties for offences under the Act where no specific penalty is provided in the Act. A person guilty of such an offence faces a fine of up to $10,000 or imprisonment for up to 3 years, with additional fines for continuing offences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it establishes penalties for data protection violations that may occur during cross-border data transfers, particularly when specific penalties are not defined.

requirements.text: A person guilty of an offence without specified penalties faces a fine of up to $10,000 or imprisonment for up to 3 years
For continuing offences, a further fine of up to $1,000 per day may be imposed

sections.source_quote: A person guilty of an offence under this Act for which no penalty is expressly provided shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $10,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or to both and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $1,000 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction.

sections.heading: No liability shall be incurred by —
sections.title: Protection from personal liability
sections.summary: This section provides immunity from liability for members, officers, and authorized personnel of relevant bodies who act with reasonable care and in good faith in the course of performing their duties under the Act. It covers actions related to the exercise of powers, performance of functions, and compliance with the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it protects individuals involved in data protection activities from personal liability, which is important when organizations transfer data internationally and involve third parties.

requirements.text: Members, officers, and authorized personnel of relevant bodies are protected from liability for actions taken with reasonable care and in good faith

sections.source_quote: No liability shall be incurred by — (a) any member or officer of a relevant body; (b) any person authorised, appointed or employed to assist a relevant body; (c) any person who is on secondment or attachment to a relevant body; (d) any person authorised or appointed by a relevant body to exercise the relevant body's powers, perform the relevant body's functions or discharge the relevant body's duties or to assist the relevant body in the exercise of its powers, the performance of its functions or the discharge of its duties under this Act or any other written law; or (e) any inspector or any person authorised, appointed or employed to assist an inspector in connection with any function or duty of the inspector under this Act, as a result of anything done (including any statement made) or omitted to be done with reasonable care and in good faith in the course of or in connection with — (f) the exercise or purported exercise of any power under this Act or any other written law; (g) the performance or purported performance of any function or the discharge or purported discharge of any duty under this Act or any other written law; or (h) the compliance or purported compliance with this Act or any other written law.
sections.relevance_reason: Immunity from liability for compliant actions

sections.heading: The Commission may, with the approval of the Minister, by order in the Gazette,
For the purposes of this Act, if any doubt arises as to whether anything is
sections.title: Certification by Minister resolves ambiguities in national interest matters
sections.summary: This section establishes that a certificate signed by the Minister responsible for a specific matter serves as conclusive evidence when there is doubt about whether something is necessary for a purpose or contrary to national interest. The certificate resolves ambiguities in legal interpretation related to national interest considerations without specifying the exact matters covered.

requirements.text: A certificate signed by the Minister charged with responsibility for that matter is conclusive evidence of matters stated in the certificate

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Act, if any doubt arises as to whether anything is necessary for the purpose of, or could be contrary to, the national interest, a certificate signed by the Minister charged with responsibility for that matter is conclusive evidence of the matters stated in the certificate.
sections.relevance_reason: Minister's certificate resolves ambiguities in national interest matters

sections.heading: Rules of Court may be made to provide for the practice and procedure relating to
sections.title: Rules of Court for data breach actions and appeals
sections.summary: This section allows Rules of Court to establish procedures for actions and appeals related to data breach investigations, including requiring claimants to notify the Commission upon initiating such actions or appeals. The rules must address notification requirements and related matters to ensure timely regulatory engagement.

requirements.text: Rules of Court may provide for practice and procedure relating to actions under section 48O and appeals under section 48R
The claimant must notify the Commission upon commencing any such action or appeal

sections.source_quote: Rules of Court may be made to provide for the practice and procedure relating to actions under section 48O and appeals under section 48R, including the requirement that the claimant notify the Commission upon commencing any such action or appeal, and for matters related thereto.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification requirement for data breach actions

sections.heading: The personal data about an individual is collected, used or disclosed (as the case may be) by the
sections.title: Employment-related data collection, use and disclosure for employment purposes
sections.summary: This section permits organisations to collect, use or disclose personal data about an individual for entering into or managing employment relationships, including appointing individuals to offices or terminating employment. The data must be reasonable for the purpose of employment relationship management. This applies to organisations engaged in employment activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows data transfers for employment purposes with specific conditions on data relevance and purpose limitation. Relevant for DTIA when handling employee data across borders.

requirements.text: Personal data about an individual is collected, used or disclosed by the organisation for entering into employment relationship
Personal data about an individual is collected, used or disclosed by the organisation for managing or terminating employment relationship

sections.source_quote: The personal data about an individual is collected, used or disclosed (as the case may be) by the organisation, and the collection, use or disclosure (as the case may be) of the personal data is reasonable for the purpose of or in relation to the organisation — (a) entering into an employment relationship with the individual or appointing the individual to any office; or (b) managing or terminating the employment relationship with or appointment of the individual.
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses employment data handling for compliance
